1: YHWH ʾôrî wĕyišʿî mimmî ʾîrā YHWH māʿôz ḥayyay mimmî ʾepḥad (YHWH ʾôrî wĕyišʿî mimmî ʾîrā YHWH māʿôz ḥayyay mimmî ʾep̄ḥaḏ): 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?' The psalm opens with a triple divine declaration: YHWH as ʾôr ('light'), yešûʿâ ('salvation'), and māʿôz ('stronghold/refuge'). The light-metaphor (ʾôr) denotes guidance, revelation, and life itself — the opposite of darkness and death (cf. Isaiah 9:2; John 8:12). The rhetorical questions mimmî ʾîrā / mimmî ʾepḥad ('whom shall I fear? / of whom shall I be afraid?') eliminate every ground for fear: if YHWH is all three, no human power can threaten what matters most.
2: biqrōb ʿālay mĕrēʿîm leʾĕkōl ʾet-bĕśārî (biqrōḇ ʿālay mĕrēʿîm leʾĕḵōl ʾeṯ-bĕśārî): 'When evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh.' The verb ʾākal ('eat') applied to flesh — leʾĕkōl ʾet-bĕśārî — is a violent metaphor for total consumption/destruction (cf. Psalms 14:4). The enemies are mĕrēʿîm ('evildoers') who approach (qārob, 'draw near') with intent to destroy. The reversal: hēmmâ kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû, 'they stumbled and fell.' The perfects are perfects of confidence — the psalmist narrates the defeat as already accomplished. The approach of enemies results in their own collapse before the one they attacked.
3: ʾim taḥăneh ʿālay maḥăneh lōʾ-yîrā libbî (ʾim taḥăneh ʿālay maḥăneh lōʾ-yîrā libbî): 'Though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear.' The hypothetical ʾim ('though, even if') introduces extreme test cases: an encamped army (maḥăneh — the same term for Israel's camp in the wilderness) and war (milḥāmâ) arrayed against the psalmist. The response of the heart (lēb): lōʾ-yîrā ('shall not fear') and lōʾ ʾeṭaḥ ('I will remain confident/trust'). The noun biṣṣōʾt ('in this' — the crisis) is the circumstance that occasions the confidence declaration: not despite the danger but precisely in it.
4: ʾaḥat šāʾaltî mēʾēt YHWH ʾôtāh ʾăbaqqēš (ʾaḥat šāʾaltî mēʾēt YHWH ʾôṯāh ʾăḇaqqēš): 'One thing have I asked of the LORD, that will I seek after.' The emphatic ʾaḥat ('one thing') narrows the entire scope of prayer to a single petition — the concentration of all desire into one longing. The petition: šibtî bĕbêt-YHWH kol-yĕmê ḥayyay lĕḥăzôt bĕnoʿam YHWH ûlĕbaqqēr bĕhêkālô, 'to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD and to inquire in his temple.' The three infinitives — šibtî ('dwelling'), lĕḥăzôt ('gazing'), ûlĕbaqqēr ('inquiring') — constitute the fullness of sanctuary life: permanence, contemplation, and seeking. The noʿam YHWH ('beauty/pleasantness of the LORD') is the ultimate object of vision (cf. Psalms 16:11; Revelation 22:4).
5: kî yiṣpĕnēnî bĕsukkōh bĕyôm rāʿâ (kî yiṣp̄ĕnēnî bĕsukkōh bĕyôm rāʿâ): 'for he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble.' The verb ṣāpan (piel, 'to hide, treasure up') pictures YHWH concealing the psalmist in sukkō ('his shelter/booth') — the divine dwelling as protective cover. The second image: yastirēnî bĕsēter ʾohŏlô, 'he will conceal me under the cover of his tent' (the tabernacle as hiding place). The third: yĕrômĕmēnî ʿal-ṣûr ('he will set me high upon a rock') — elevation beyond the reach of enemies. The day of trouble (yôm rāʿâ) does not nullify the confidence of Psalms 27:1-3 but confirms its necessity.
6: wĕʿattâ yārûm rōʾšî ʿal ʾōyĕbay sĕbîbôtay (wĕʿattâ yārûm rōʾšî ʿal ʾōyĕbay sĕḇîḇôṯay): 'And now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me.' The raising of the head (yārûm rōʾšî) is the reversal of the bowing/humiliation the enemies sought. The vow that follows: wĕʾezbeḥâ bĕʾohŏlô zibbĕḥê tĕrûʿâ ʾāšîrâ waʾăzammĕrâ laYHWH, 'I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make melody to the LORD.' The sacrifices of tĕrûʿâ ('shouting, alarm/joy') transform military victory into liturgical offering: the battle's outcome becomes the occasion for worship.
7: šmaʿ YHWH qôlî ʾeqrāʾ wĕḥonnēnî waʿănēnî (šmaʿ YHWH qôlî ʾeqrāʾ wĕḥonnēnî waʿănēnî): 'Hear, O LORD, when I cry aloud; be gracious to me and answer me.' The psalm's second half (Psalms 27:7-14) shifts from confidence declaration to active prayer — suggesting the psalm was composed in two movements or that the confident first section empowers the petition of the second. The trio šmaʿ ('hear!'), ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!'), and waʿănēnî ('answer me!') are the classic lament imperatives. Even confident trust expresses itself in explicit petition.
8: lĕkā ʾāmar libbî baqqĕšû pānāy ʾet-pānêkā YHWH ʾăbaqqēš (lĕḵā ʾāmar libbî baqqĕšû p̄ānāy ʾeṯ-p̄āneḵā YHWH ʾăḇaqqēš): 'You have said, 'Seek my face.' My heart says to you, 'Your face, LORD, do I seek.'' The divine imperative baqqĕšû pānāy ('seek my face!') — whether heard in Scripture, liturgy, or internal conviction — is met with immediate response: ʾeṯ-pānêkā YHWH ʾăbaqqēš ('your face, O LORD, I seek'). The two-part structure of divine invitation and human response is the grammar of prayer: God's call precedes and elicits the seeking. The pānîm ('face') — divine self-disclosure — is both the command's content and the prayer's object.
9: ʾal-tastēr pānêkā mimmennî ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ʿabdekā (ʾal-tastēr p̄āneḵā mimmennî ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ʿaḇḏeḵā): 'Hide not your face from me; turn not your servant away in anger.' The negative imperatives — ʾal-tastēr ('do not hide!') and ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ('do not turn away in anger!') — are prayers against divine wrath and hiddenness. The psalmist has been seeking the face (Psalms 27:8) and now fears the face might be hidden — the very hiding that Psalms 22:1 lamented. The continued address ʿabdekā ('your servant') maintains the relational framework: servant-speech is the human position before the sovereign YHWH. The phrase ʾĕzrātî hāyîtā, 'you have been my help,' appeals to prior experience to ground present petition.
10: kî-ʾābî wĕʾimmî ʿăzābûnî waYHWH yaʾasĕpēnî (kî-ʾāḇî wĕʾimmî ʿăzāḇûnî waYHWH yaʾasĕp̄ēnî): 'For my father and my mother have forsaken me, but the LORD will take me in.' The extreme hypothetical — parental abandonment — represents the deepest human betrayal: even the most natural and binding of bonds may fail. The response waYHWH yaʾasĕpēnî, 'but the LORD will take me in/gather me' — from ʾāsap ('gather, receive') — images YHWH as the one who gathers the abandoned child. The statement grounds the appeal of Psalms 27:9: if even parents might fail, YHWH's faithfulness must not. This is the Psalter's most poignant expression of YHWH as the ultimate parent (cf. Isaiah 49:15).
11: hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ûnaḥēnî bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy (hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ûnaḥēnî bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy): 'Teach me your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path because of my enemies.' The request hôrēnî ('teach me!') from yārâ ('to throw, point, teach') — the root of tôrâ — asks for foundational instruction in divine direction. The phrase bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr ('on a level/even path') echoes Psalms 25:21 (tōm) and Psalms 26:12 (mîšôr): moral levelness as protection from stumbling. The enemies are the context: lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy, 'because of my enemies/those who watch me,' who would exploit any deviation.
12: ʾal-tittĕnēnî bĕnepeš ṣārāy (ʾal-tittĕnēnî bĕnepeš ṣārāy): 'Give me not up to the will of my adversaries.' The phrase bĕnepeš ṣārāy ('into the desire/appetite of my adversaries') — nepeš as 'appetite, desire' (rather than 'soul') — means: do not let them have what they hunger for, which is my destruction. The motive clause kî qāmû bî ʿēdê šāqer wîpēaḥ ḥāmās, 'for false witnesses have risen against me, and they breathe out violence,' specifies the judicial danger: perjury in court. ʿēdê šāqer ('false witnesses') is a specific violation of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:16) — the psalmist faces coordinated legal assault backed by lies.
13: lûlēʾ heʾĕmantî lirʾôt bĕṭûb YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm (lûlēʾ heʾĕmantî lirʾôt bĕṭûḇ YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm): 'I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living.' The phrase lûlēʾ ('unless, if not') begins a conditional whose main clause is suppressed — a classical rhetorical aposiopesis: 'If I had not believed... [I would have despaired].' What remains is the content of the faith: lirʾôt bĕṭûb YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm, 'to see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living.' The ṭûb YHWH ('goodness of the LORD') is the object of faith-based vision — not merely hoped for in the afterlife but expected in this life. ʾereṣ ḥayyîm ('land of the living') = the present world of embodied existence, not the realm of the dead.
14: qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕkā wĕqawwēh ʾel-YHWH (qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕḵā wĕqawwēh ʾel-YHWH): 'Wait for the LORD; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the LORD!' The double imperative qawwēh ... qawwēh ('wait ... wait') — from qāwâ ('to wait with taut, rope-like expectation') — frames the verse as an inclusio of hope. Between the two waits: ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕkā ('be strong and let your heart take courage') — the verbs used for Joshua's commissioning (Joshua 1:6-9) and David's charge to Solomon (1 Chronicles 22:13). The courageous heart is not self-manufactured but the result of waiting on YHWH — confidence drawn from the one waited for. The psalm ends not with resolution but with the ongoing posture of expectation.
